section.title: Godliness with Contentment
section.sectionRationale: Paul addresses servants and masters, then exposes false teachers who treat godliness as a way to get rich. Over against them he sets the true wealth: godliness with contentment is great gain. We brought nothing into the world and can take nothing out, so those who crave riches fall into ruin, for the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil.
section.thesis: Real gain is not money but godliness joined to contentment; the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil that pierces the greedy with many sorrows, while the godly rest content in God with food and covering.
section.frameNote: Three movements: honorable service under masters; the exposure of teachers who see godliness as a means of gain; and the great gain of godliness with contentment against the peril of loving money.

units.label: Serving with honor
units.note: so God's name is not discredited

verses.text: All who are under the {{zygos|yoke}} of slavery should regard their masters as fully worthy of honor, so that God’s name and our teaching will not be discredited.

Those who have believing masters should not show disrespect because they are brothers, but should serve them all the more, since those receiving their good service are beloved believers. Teach and encourage these principles.

units.label: Reject false doctrines
units.note: godliness is not a way to get rich

verses.text: If anyone teaches another doctrine and disagrees with the sound words of our Lord Jesus Christ and with godly teaching,
he is conceited and understands nothing. Instead, he has an unhealthy interest in controversies and disputes about words, out of which come envy, strife, abusive talk, evil suspicions,
and constant friction between men of depraved mind who are devoid of the truth. These men regard godliness as a means of {{porismos|gain}}.
Of course, {{eusebeia|godliness}} with {{autarkeia|contentment}} is great gain.
For we brought nothing into the world, so we cannot carry anything out of it.
But if we have food and clothing, we will be content with these.
Those who want to be rich, however, fall into temptation and become ensnared by many foolish and harmful desires that plunge them into ruin and destruction.
For the {{philargyria|love of money}} is the root of all kinds of evil. By craving it, some have wandered away from the faith and pierced themselves with many sorrows.

zygos.gloss: A yoke, the wooden bar laid across the neck of working animals, and by extension the burden of servitude. Bondservants under the yoke of slavery are to honor their masters so that the gospel is not slandered, showing the transforming power of faith even in hard circumstances.

porismos.gloss: A means of gain, a way of making money. The false teachers treat godliness as porismos, a business to enrich themselves. Paul overturns the word in verse 6: godliness truly is great gain, but joined to contentment, not to greed.

eusebeia.gloss: Godliness, reverence for God lived out, a key word of the Pastoral Epistles. The false teachers prize it only as a path to profit, but real godliness, paired with contentment, is the great gain, valuable now and forever, unlike money that cannot be carried out of the world.

autarkeia.gloss: Contentment, self-sufficiency, a settled satisfaction independent of circumstances. For Paul it is not stoic self-reliance but rest in God's provision, being satisfied with food and covering. Joined to godliness, it turns a modest life into great gain.

philargyria.gloss: The love of money, literally love of silver. Paul calls it a root of all kinds of evil. It is not money itself but the craving for it that leads people astray, luring some away from the faith and piercing them with many sorrows.

basic.context: Paul tells Christian servants to honor their masters so the gospel is not discredited, and to serve believing masters all the more. He then exposes false teachers who are proud, quarrelsome, and see godliness as a way to make money. Against them he sets the truth: godliness with contentment is great gain. We come with nothing and leave with nothing, so craving riches leads to ruin, because the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil.
basic.hermeneutics: The instruction to servants speaks into a real social setting; its enduring principle is that faith transforms how we work and relate, guarding the gospel's reputation. The teaching on money is directly prescriptive: contentment is the mark of godliness, and the love of money is a spiritual danger to be fled.
basic.originalLanguages: Servants under the zygos, yoke, honor their masters. False teachers treat godliness as porismos, a means of gain, but real eusebeia, godliness, with autarkeia, contentment, is great gain. The philargyria, love of money, is a root of all kinds of evil.
basic.typology: Contentment with food and covering recalls Israel gathering daily manna, trusting God's provision, and points to Christ who bids us seek first the kingdom and trust our Father for daily bread. The true riches are found in Him.
basic.christ: Sound teaching is measured by the words of our Lord Jesus Christ. He, though rich, became poor so that we through His poverty might become rich, embodying the great gain of godliness that no money can buy. To wander after wealth is to wander from the faith that is in Him.
basic.god: God's name is at stake in how His people work and handle money. He is the giver of every good thing and the true treasure that makes contentment possible. He warns that the love of money competes with love for Him and pierces the heart that chases it.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 13 says be content with what you have, for God will never leave you. Matthew 6 warns you cannot serve God and money. Philippians 4 tells of learning contentment in every circumstance.
basic.oneStory: From manna in the wilderness to the treasure of the kingdom, God has taught His people to trust Him rather than riches. Here He exposes greed dressed as religion and calls His people to contentment in Him. It is part of His long work of freeing hearts from idols to find their gain in Christ.

context.literary: The final chapter opens with instructions to servants, then returns to the false teachers introduced in chapter 1, now unmasked as greedy. This pivots into the great contrast of the letter's close: godliness with contentment against the love of money, which prepares the personal charge to Timothy that follows in verses 11 and following.
context.historical: Slavery was woven through the Roman economy, and many early Christians were bondservants. Paul's counsel aims to protect the gospel's witness while elsewhere he undermines slavery's roots, as in Philemon. Teachers who peddled religion for profit were common, and Ephesus was a wealthy commercial city where the love of money was a live temptation.
context.canonical: The teaching joins the Bible's steady witness against greed and for contentment, from the wisdom literature to Jesus' warnings about mammon. Verse 10 is among the most quoted, and most misquoted, verses on money in Scripture.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To guard the gospel's witness in the workplace, to expose teachers who use religion for profit, and to set before the church the true wealth of godliness with contentment over against the ruin brought by the love of money.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The counsel to servants addresses a specific institution, and its enduring principle, that faith reshapes how we serve and protects the gospel's name, applies broadly to work and authority today. The teaching on contentment and the danger of loving money is directly prescriptive for every believer.

debates.question: Does verse 1 endorse slavery?

views.label: Pastoral counsel within a fallen system
views.case: Paul addresses believers already in bondage, teaching them to adorn the gospel by honorable conduct, not endorsing slavery as a good, while elsewhere sowing seeds that undo it, Philemon and Galatians 3:28.

views.label: A witness aimed at the gospel's honor
views.case: The stated goal is that God's name and the teaching not be blasphemed; the focus is protecting the church's testimony before a watching, hostile world.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Paul is not commending slavery but directing Christians to live faithfully where they are, in a way that commends rather than discredits the gospel.

debates.question: Is money itself the root of all evil?

views.label: The love of money, not money
views.case: Paul is precise: it is philargyria, the craving for money, that is a root of all kinds of evil, not wealth in itself, which verses 17 to 19 treat as a stewardship.

views.label: A root, among others, of many evils
views.case: The phrase is a root of all kinds of evil, not the single root of every evil; greed breeds a wide range of sins and leads some away from the faith.

debates.bearing: Both guard against the common misquotation: the peril is the love of money, and it is one deep root from which many evils grow.

study.originalLanguages: Bondservants under the zygos, the yoke of slavery, are to honor their masters so the gospel is not slandered. The false teachers regard godliness as porismos, a means of gain, a business to line their pockets. Paul seizes the word and turns it: eusebeia, godliness, joined to autarkeia, contentment, a settled satisfaction resting in God's provision, is itself the great gain. The peril is philargyria, love of silver, which he names a root of all kinds of evil; craving it, some have wandered from the faith and impaled themselves on many sorrows.

typology.fulfillment: Recalls Israel's daily manna and points to Christ's call to trust the Father for daily bread and seek first the kingdom.

typology.type: The true riches over earthly wealth
typology.fulfillment: Found in Christ, who became poor to make us rich, 2 Corinthians 8:9, the great gain that money cannot buy.

study.christ: The measuring line for sound teaching is the sound words of our Lord Jesus Christ, so any doctrine that fails His words is false, however profitable. Christ Himself is the pattern of the great gain Paul commends: though He was rich, He became poor so that we through His poverty might become rich. To chase money is to wander from the faith that is in Him, while contentment flows from finding in Christ a treasure the world can neither give nor take away.
study.god: God's own name is bound up with how His people work and handle wealth; poor conduct discredits Him, faithful conduct commends Him. He is the generous giver who makes contentment possible, the true treasure beside whom money is a rival and a counterfeit. He warns that loving money leads the heart away from Him into ruin, for no one can serve two masters.

crossReferences.note: Keep your lives free from the love of money and be content, for God will never leave you.
You cannot serve both God and money, the rivalry behind the love of money.
Paul learned to be content in any circumstance through Christ who strengthens him.
Though He was rich, Christ became poor, so that we through His poverty might become rich.

study.oneStory: From manna in the wilderness to Jesus' warnings about mammon, God has taught His people to trust Him rather than riches. Here He unmasks greed disguised as godliness and calls His people to contentment in Him. Freeing hearts from the idol of money to find their gain in Christ is part of God's long work of forming a people whose treasure is in heaven.

quiz.q: Why are Christian servants told to honor their masters?

options.text: So that God's name and the church's teaching will not be discredited.
options.note: Verse 1 gives the gospel's reputation as the reason.

options.text: Because slavery is a good and permanent institution.
options.note: Paul addresses believers within the system, not commending it; the aim is the gospel's witness.

options.text: Because masters always deserve it on their own merits.
options.note: The motive given is the honor of God's name, not the masters' merit.

options.text: So they can earn their freedom by good behavior.
options.note: The stated goal is the gospel's reputation, not earning freedom.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe teachers who use religion for profit?

options.text: Conceited, quarrelsome, and regarding godliness as a means of gain.
options.note: Verses 4 and 5 expose their pride and greed.

options.text: Wise leaders worth imitating.
options.note: They are the opposite of what to imitate; Paul condemns them.

options.text: Sincere but slightly mistaken.
options.note: Paul calls them depraved in mind and devoid of the truth, not merely mistaken.

options.text: Harmless eccentrics.
options.note: They breed envy, strife, and friction; they are far from harmless.

quiz.q: What does Paul say is great gain?

options.text: Godliness with contentment.
options.note: Verse 6 names godliness joined to contentment as great gain.

options.text: Godliness that produces wealth.
options.note: That is the false teachers' view; Paul pairs godliness with contentment, not riches.

options.text: Wealth accumulated through hard work.
options.note: We can carry nothing out; wealth is not the gain Paul commends.

options.text: Freedom from all responsibility.
options.note: The gain is godliness with contentment, not escape from duty.

quiz.q: What reason does Paul give for contentment in verses 7 and 8?

options.text: We brought nothing into the world and can carry nothing out; food and clothing suffice.
options.note: Verses 7 and 8 ground contentment in this reality.

options.text: Because possessions last forever.
options.note: The opposite; we cannot take anything out, which is why we should be content.

options.text: Because poverty is holier than provision.
options.note: Paul is content with food and clothing, not idealizing poverty itself.

options.text: Because God does not care about our needs.
options.note: God provides; contentment rests on His provision, not His indifference.

quiz.q: What does Paul say about the love of money?

options.text: It is a root of all kinds of evil that has led some away from the faith.
options.note: Verse 10 names the love of money, not money itself, as the danger.

options.text: Money itself is the single root of every evil.
options.note: A common misquotation; Paul specifies the love of money and a root of all kinds of evil.

options.text: Wealth is always sinful to possess.
options.note: The peril is the craving for money; verses 17 to 19 treat wealth as a stewardship.

options.text: Money has no spiritual danger at all.
options.note: Paul warns it can pierce people with many sorrows and draw them from the faith.

questions: Paul says our work can either commend or discredit the gospel. How does your conduct at work reflect on the God you claim to serve?
The false teachers saw godliness as a way to get rich. Where might you be tempted to treat faith as a means to some other gain?
Godliness with contentment is great gain. What would genuine contentment with food and covering look like in your life this year?
We bring nothing in and take nothing out. How does that reality reshape the way you hold your possessions and plans?
The love of money pierces people with many sorrows. Where do you sense that craving pulling at your heart, and how will you flee it?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that some manuscripts add Withdraw yourself from such, a later reading not in the earliest texts.
The BSB footnote records the variant so certainly for so, a minor difference among manuscript families.
The love of money renders philargyria, love of silver; Paul says it is a root of all kinds of evil, not that money itself is the root of every evil.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Timothy 6:1-10 this session. No em or en dashes occur in this passage.
